title: Rotate Image
description:
`matrix` is a square n x n grid of integers representing a picture. Turn that picture 90 degrees clockwise without allocating a second n x n grid to hold the result -- the rotation should happen using the original matrix's own storage -- and hand back the rotated grid.

**Example**

```
matrix = [
  [1, 2, 3],
  [4, 5, 6],
  [7, 8, 9]
]  ->  [
  [7, 4, 1],
  [8, 5, 2],
  [9, 6, 3]
]
```
starter_code:
def rotate(matrix):
    # matrix: n x n list of lists of int. Rotate 90 degrees clockwise in place
    # and return the rotated matrix.
    pass
hints: A 90 degree clockwise rotation can be achieved by first transposing the matrix (swap matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i]) and then reversing each row.
Do the transpose in place by only swapping the upper triangle with the lower triangle, then reverse each row's elements to finish the rotation.

solutions.name: Transpose then reverse rows
solutions.explanation: Transposing an n x n matrix (reflecting across the main diagonal, swapping matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i] for i < j) turns rows into columns. Reversing every row afterward turns those columns into the clockwise-rotated rows. Both steps operate directly on the input matrix, so no extra n x n buffer is needed.
